description: Figure 13 presents nanoindentation results for peak-aged Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with varying Al contents: 15Al aged at 600 °C for 6 h, 17Al aged at 575 °C for 6 h, and 19Al aged at 600 °C for 10 h. Panel (a) shows load–displacement curves for the three alloys, reporting average nano-hardness values of 11.7 ± 0.4 GPa, 11.1 ± 0.6 GPa, and 10.4 ± 1.3 GPa, respectively, where both the mean hardness and curve-to-curve variability trend downward with increasing Al. The accompanying secondary electron (SE) micrographs display arrays of nanoindentation impressions on 15Al, 17Al, and 19Al, each annotated with local hardness readings and a 2 μm scale bar. The individual indent hardnesses span roughly 10.9–12.4 GPa for 15Al, 10.2–12.3 GPa for 17Al, and 8.0–12.9 GPa for 19Al, revealing progressively broader distributions as Al rises. This increasing scatter reflects the greater volume fraction of softer FCC Cu-rich and BCC precipitates formed between β-Mn interfaces at higher Al contents. Collectively, the data support the conclusion that although elevated Al marginally raises the intrinsic hardness of β-Mn through solid-solution strengthening, the overall micro-hardness decreases with increasing Al because the growing proportion of softer interfacial precipitates reduces the absolute β-Mn volume fraction and introduces larger property fluctuations.
